claim: `dirname(dir)` is called OUTSIDE the try/catch on line 15; when the IPC handler in register-orky.ts passes a non-string `cwd` (TypeScript annotations are compile-time only — runtime IPC args arrive as `unknown`), `join(dir, '.orky')` throws a TypeError that IS caught, but the immediately following `dirname(dir)` call throws an uncaught TypeError that propagates up as a rejected Promise. The caller (`watch()`) is invoked with `void` (`void orky.watch(id, cwd)`) so the rejection is unhandled. Electron 33 ships Node.js 22 which exits the main process on unhandled rejections by default (`--unhandled-rejections=throw`). An attacker who can reach the `ipcRenderer.send` layer (e.g., DevTools console, compromised preload) can terminate the app with a single message.
recommendation: Add a runtime string guard at the IPC boundary in `register-orky.ts`: `if (typeof cwd !== 'string' || typeof id !== 'string') return;` before `orky.watch(id, cwd)`. Additionally, wrap the `dirname(dir)` call inside `findOrkyRoot` in its own try/catch, or guard with `if (typeof dir !== 'string') break;` after the try block so a non-string initial value degrades gracefully to `null` rather than throwing.
resolution: FIXED+verified: findOrkyRoot non-string-safe (returns null); orky:watch/unwatch guard non-string id/cwd; TEST-044/054.

claim: `ipcMain.on(CH.orkyWatch, ...)` and `ipcMain.on(CH.orkyUnwatch, ...)` are registered once per window (called from `registerHandlers` which is invoked per `BrowserWindow`). Because `ipcMain.on` is process-global, every registered handler fires for every matching message from any window. With N open windows, N `OrkyTracker` instances each receive the `orky:watch` event from any single sender — including pane IDs that belong to a different window. Consequences: (1) N-1 spurious FS watchers are opened against the path; (2) each tracker emits `orky:status` to ITS OWN window, so Window B receives status pushes for Window A's pane IDs (cross-window status disclosure); (3) an `orky:unwatch` from any window cancels the tracker in all windows, silently killing status for panes that were watching correctly. `register-pty.ts` avoids this via `claimPane(paneId, e.sender)` ownership validation; the orky handler has no equivalent guard.
recommendation: Capture the event sender and scope each handler to its own window: `ipcMain.on(CH.orkyWatch, (e, id, cwd) => { if (BrowserWindow.fromWebContents(e.sender) === win) { void orky.watch(id, cwd) } })`. Alternatively, register and deregister named handler functions in `registerOrky` so that the handler is truly per-window scoped (use `ipcMain.removeListener` in the disposer). As a minimum, validate that `id` is a pane ID that belongs to the sender's window before acting on it.
resolution: FIXED+verified: registerOrky(send,win) ownsSender check; disposer removeListener; TEST-054.

claim: `readdir(join(sess.orkyDir, 'features'))` returns all entries with no count cap, and the subsequent `for` loop sequentially `await`s two `readFile` calls per entry with no file-size limit. An adversarial `.orky/features/` tree with thousands of directories (easily created by a script running in a terminal pane, or present in a malicious repo) causes the re-read path to issue thousands of async I/O operations in sequence during a single debounced event. While these are truly async (non-blocking), the sheer volume can hold the tracker's `reread` coroutine occupied for seconds, delay status updates, and allocate heap proportional to the sum of all JSON file sizes simultaneously held in the parse pipeline. A single crafted `state.json` or `findings.json` of several hundred MiB passes through `readFile` → `JSON.parse` → `normalizeFeatureRaw` with no size guard, creating a GiB-class allocation in the main process heap.
recommendation: Apply a feature-directory count cap (e.g., `slugs = slugs.slice(0, 200)` with a warning log) and a per-file size cap before parsing (check `stat.size` before `readFile`, skip and warn if above a threshold such as 1 MiB). Add a comment referencing the DoS risk so reviewers understand the intentional ceiling.
resolution: FIXED+verified: readdir capped 200; readJson stat-size-cap 1MiB BEFORE readFile; TEST-045/046.

claim: `readFile(join(fdir, 'state.json'), 'utf8')` and the equivalent call for `findings.json` follow symlinks transparently. If an attacker who can write into `.orky/features/` places a symbolic link (`features/evil -> /attacker/controlled/dir/`), `join(fdir, 'state.json')` resolves to `/attacker/controlled/dir/state.json` and the file is read. The content is parsed and surfaced as Orky status in the UI. While React escapes text content (preventing classic HTML injection), attacker-controlled strings can appear as feature names, phase labels, escalation IDs, and `reason` fields rendered in the popover and chip, enabling misleading status display (e.g., a fabricated `pipeline complete` indicator while critical findings exist) and unlimited-length strings allocated in the main-process heap.
recommendation: After resolving the feature directory entry path, call `fs.promises.realpath(fdir)` and verify the resolved path is still inside `sess.orkyDir` before reading any files beneath it. Alternatively, use `fs.promises.lstat` on the entry and skip it if `isSymbolicLink()` returns true, logging a warning.
resolution: FIXED (dir-level): lstat symlink skip on feature dirs; TEST-047 (host-gated). Residual file-level symlink tracked as FINDING-SEC-006 (LOW).

claim: `document.querySelector(\`[data-testid="orky-chip-${paneId}"]\`)` interpolates `paneId` directly into a CSS selector string without escaping. CSS attribute selector syntax reserves `"`, `]`, `\`, and several other characters. If a pane ID ever contains one of these characters the call throws a `DOMException: SyntaxError` that is unhandled (no try/catch around the call), crashing the `useLayoutEffect` and leaving the popover in the `!pos` state (renders nothing). Pane IDs are currently UUIDs, so this is not immediately exploitable, but the defense rests on an undocumented invariant that is not enforced at the IPC or store layer.
recommendation: Replace the raw interpolation with `CSS.escape(paneId)`: `document.querySelector(\`[data-testid="orky-chip-${CSS.escape(paneId)}"]\`)`. This is a one-character change that makes the selector safe regardless of the pane ID format and removes the dependency on the UUID invariant.
resolution: FIXED+verified: CSS.escape(paneId) in popover selector; TEST-050.

claim: Zero test files were added in this commit. The spec DoD mandates vitest unit tests for all pure mappers (ORKY_PHASES/gate N/M, openBlockingCount, isStalled, orkyFeatureStatus, selectChipFeature, chip label, orkyPaneStatus determinism, CONV-002 edge cases) AND Playwright e2e tests with a synthetic .orky/ fixture. None exist. CLAUDE.md requires TDD ('Write the failing test first'). The injectable now/thresholdMs/debounceMs on OrkyTracker is wired for testability but never exercised.
recommendation: Add tests/unit/orky-status.test.ts covering every REQ acceptance criterion from the spec (REQ-001..REQ-009, REQ-019, REQ-021). Add tests/e2e/orky-status.spec.ts with a synthetic .orky/ fixture asserting chip/border appearance, tab-badge opt-in, cwd-leave teardown, and read-only invariant. Match the style of the existing chip-status.test.ts and status-state-machine tests.
resolution: FALSE POSITIVE (orchestrator-dismissed): reviewer was scoped to the 6d52ee9..HEAD implementation diff; the 38 frozen feature tests exist in base commit 6d52ee9 (the tests-phase freeze) and pass GREEN in the implement gate (729 tests, exit 0). Not a real defect.

claim: OrkyTracker.unwatch() is missing the early-return guard that UsageTracker.unwatch() has (`if (!this.sessions.has(id)) return`). Without it, calling unwatch() for a pane that was never watched (or was already unwatched) falls through to `this.emit(id, null)`, sending a spurious `orky:status(id, null)` IPC push to the renderer. This deviates from the declared clone obligation and emits unnecessary IPC traffic; the store's setOrky delete-non-existent-key is a no-op but it still dispatches a state update.
recommendation: Add `if (!this.sessions.has(id)) return` at the top of unwatch(), matching UsageTracker exactly.
resolution: FIXED: unwatch early-return guard added.

claim: isStalled() calls normalizeFeatureRaw(feature) internally (line 109) even though its parameter is typed OrkyFeatureRaw — an already-normalized shape. Every real call site (orkyFeatureStatus at line 133) normalizes the input BEFORE passing it. The double normalization wastes CPU on every stall check and is semantically contradictory: the type signature says 'already safe,' the body says 'distrust the caller.' It also makes isStalled harder to reason about in isolation tests.
recommendation: Remove the internal normalizeFeatureRaw call in isStalled; the function should trust its typed parameter. If defensive normalization for direct external callers is desired, document it in the type or use `unknown` as the parameter type instead.
resolution: FIXED: removed double-normalize in isStalled.

claim: findOrkyRoot uses statSync (blocking synchronous I/O) and is called directly from the async OrkyTracker.watch() method before the session slot is claimed. On slow or networked filesystems, an 8-level statSync walk blocks the Node.js main-process event loop for the duration, stalling all other IPC handlers. The rest of the tracker exclusively uses fs/promises for all I/O (TASK-009 constraint: 'read via fs/promises ONLY'), making this the one synchronous outlier.
recommendation: Convert findOrkyRoot to an async function using `import { stat } from 'node:fs/promises'` and `await stat(...)`. Update the call in OrkyTracker.watch() to `await findOrkyRoot(...)` (still before the session-slot claim, since findOrkyRoot doesn't await after the slot is needed).

claim: orkyChipStatus() and the OrkyChipStatus interface are exported from chip-status.ts but are never imported or used anywhere in the codebase. The MinimizedTray chip (MinimizedTray.tsx) still only calls chipStatus() with byte-derived status — it renders no Orky state at all. Plan TASK-008 explicitly says the Orky chip-status variant was introduced so 'the toolbar chip AND the minimized-tray chip can render an Orky run's kind.' The toolbar chip reads OrkyPaneStatus directly without going through orkyChipStatus(). The abstraction was written but left wired to nothing.
recommendation: Either (a) wire orkyChipStatus into MinimizedTray.MinChip (read s.orky[paneId], call orkyChipStatus when present, blend the result into the chip icon/label/data-status) to complete the TASK-008 intent; or (b) remove the dead function if the decision is to not render Orky state in the minimized tray. Do not leave it exported and unused.
resolution: FIXED: orkyChipStatus wired into MinimizedTray chip (toolbar AND tray reuse).

claim: OrkyFeatureStatus has a `reason: OrkyReason` field not declared in the spec's explicit public interface (02-spec.md lines 52-65). The spec omits it; the implementation adds it as an undocumented extension. It is load-bearing for selectChipFeature's REASON_RANK comparator, but it appears only in the implementation-derived types — not surfaced in the spec, making spec↔type drift hard to detect in future reviews.
recommendation: Add `reason: OrkyReason` to the spec's OrkyFeatureStatus interface definition and note it is the structured discriminator used by the REQ-007 selector. This removes the spec/type drift without changing any code.
resolution: FIXED: reason:OrkyReason now declared in spec/types (drift closed).

claim: The isStalled call in orkyFeatureStatus passes dual-null for the non-active case: `isStalled(isActive ? f.feature : null, f, isActive ? lastTickAt : null, now, thresholdMs)`. isStalled already fully gates on `activeFeatureSlug === null` at its first line (returns false immediately), making the `isActive ? lastTickAt : null` ternary redundant defensive coding that slightly obscures intent — a reader must trace both arguments to confirm they're consistent.
recommendation: Simplify to `isStalled(isActive ? f.feature : null, f, lastTickAt, now, thresholdMs)`. The function's own guard handles the non-active case regardless of lastTickAt's value.

claim: The flagship 'needs a human at human-review' trigger keys on `f.phase === 'human-review'` (state.json.phase), but the REAL Orky pipeline NEVER sets state.json.phase to 'human-review'. Ground truth in this very repo: all three completed features (0001/0002/0003) terminate at state.json.phase === 'doc-sync' while their human-review gate is recorded externally (`gates['human-review'].passed=true, external:true`) WITHOUT advancing the phase. Therefore, during the window a run is actually waiting for human approval, state.json.phase is still 'doc-sync', `inHumanReview` is false, `needsHuman` is false, `kind` is 'idle' — no needs-input border, no 🔔 tab badge. The single most important thing this feature exists to surface (a run blocked on you) silently never fires.
recommendation: Do not detect awaiting-human via phase-string equality. Derive it from gate topology — the pipeline is at human-review when `gates['doc-sync'].passed === true` AND `gates['human-review']` is absent/unpassed (and not failed) — and/or use the live `active.json.phase`, which the tracker currently reads but discards. Add a fixture whose state.json reproduces the real shape (phase='doc-sync', human-review gate pending) and assert needsHuman/reason:'human-review'.
resolution: FIXED+verified vs ground truth: needs-human now gate-derived (autonomous gates through doc-sync passed AND human-review gate not passed); TEST-008/009 pin the real on-disk shape.

claim: The live phase is taken from per-feature state.json.phase, which lags the authoritative active.json.phase — and the tracker reads active.json.phase only to throw it away (it keeps slug + lastTickAt). Right now in THIS repo: active.json.phase='review' and lastTickAt is live, but feature 0004's state.json.phase='implement' with gates['implement'].passed=true. `isExecuting` (gate-of-current-phase undefined) therefore returns false, so the actively-running feature reports kind:'idle', phase 'implement', 5/8 — it is actually busy in review. Worse, because isExecuting is false, `isStalled` short-circuits to false: a hang during review/doc-sync (the exact long phases) can never light a stall, defeating REQ-003 for everything after the implement gate passes.
recommendation: Treat active.json.phase as the live phase for the active feature: compute executing/busy and stall-eligibility from active.json.phase (cross-checked against gates) rather than the lagging state.json.phase. Pass active.phase into orkyFeatureStatus for the active feature. Add a fixture reproducing the implement-gate-passed-but-active.phase='review' divergence.
resolution: FIXED+verified: live phase = active.json.phase for active feature (tracker passes activePhase), gateFrontier for non-active; 0004 renders busy/review correctly.

claim: REQ-020 says the popover MUST list non-Idle features defined as '(busy / needs-input / done-with-open-items / failed)' — plain done features with no open items are excluded. The implementation filters with `f.kind !== 'idle'`, which includes EVERY done feature regardless of open items. In this repo that means an Orky pane's popover permanently lists 0001, 0002, 0003 (all done, zero open items) and grows with every merged feature forever — a wall of stale 'done' rows implying attention is needed where none is. This contradicts the REQ-020 enumeration.
recommendation: Filter to `f.kind !== 'idle' && !(f.kind === 'done' && f.openBlocking === 0)` so a clean done feature drops out of the roll-up; keep done-with-open-items. Add a test: a roll-up of two done features (one with openBlocking>0, one with 0) lists only the former.
resolution: FIXED+verified: popover inPopover() excludes clean-done; TEST-018 pins exclusion.

claim: `lastActivityAt` is set to the heartbeat only for the active feature; every non-active feature gets 0. The single per-project heartbeat means at most one feature ever has a nonzero activity. So REQ-007's 'most-recent activity' tiebreak (step 3) is inoperative in production for the common multi-feature roll-up: among several non-active needs-you features (e.g. two finished features each carrying an open escalation), the selector falls straight through to feature-id ascending and surfaces the LOWEST-id (typically the OLDEST) feature, not the most-recently-active one. The chip can therefore point you at a stale escalation while a fresher one waits.
recommendation: Source lastActivityAt from each feature's own latest evidence — the max `gates[*].at` (and escalation timestamps) in state.json, which are already on disk — falling back to the heartbeat for the active feature. Then the recency tiebreak actually orders non-active features.
resolution: FIXED+verified: lastActivityAt now sourced from max(gates[*].at) for all features (not heartbeat-only); confirmed by determinism re-review.

claim: A NON-active feature whose state.json.phase is set with that phase's gate still undefined (i.e. it was abandoned mid-phase when the active feature switched) reads kind:'busy' indefinitely. There is no per-feature heartbeat, so nothing can ever move it out of 'busy' — the roll-up/popover shows a dead feature as 'running' forever, the opposite of the design's premise that only the active feature has a live signal.
recommendation: A feature that is not the active feature has no proof of life: cap non-active features to idle/last-gate state (never 'busy'). Reserve 'busy' for the active feature with a live heartbeat. Add a fixture: non-active feature with an in-flight (gate-undefined) phase → idle, not busy.
resolution: FIXED+verified: non-active features derive kind from gateFrontier and are never busy; confirmed by devils-advocate re-review.

claim: The ancestor walk is capped at 8 levels. A pane whose cwd is more than 8 directories below the project root (deep monorepo / nested package) yields findOrkyRoot===null, so the tracker emits a cleared status and the pane silently shows NO Orky chrome even though it is inside an Orky project. The status disappears purely as a function of cwd depth — a confusing, location-dependent blank.
recommendation: Either raise the cap meaningfully or stop on a project-root marker (.git) rather than a fixed depth, and document the bound in the feature doc. A cwd-depth-only cause of 'no Orky status' should be impossible within a normal repo checkout.

claim: REQ-006's failure border is invisible for the canonical failed case. A halted-gate failed feature maps to kind 'idle' (src/shared/orky-status.ts:147), so PaneTile composes the classes `term-status term-idle term-failure`. The new `.mosaic-window.term-failure .mosaic-window-toolbar { background-color: var(--status-failure) }` rule (index.css:55) is NOT !important, but the idle toolbar rule `.mosaic-window:not(.term-busy):not(.term-needs-input) .mosaic-window-toolbar { background: var(--panel) !important }` (index.css:71) matches the same pane (it is neither busy nor needs-input) and wins via !important. The red failure accent therefore renders ONLY when the pane is simultaneously busy or needs-input (the non-idle states), never for the pure failed-and-idle pane — which is exactly the state a halted gate failure produces. REQ-006 says failure MUST surface as the failure border treatment; here it surfaces nowhere visible, and no test asserts the rendered red (REQ-006 is covered only by the pure-mapper TEST-011).
recommendation: Make the term-failure background win for idle panes: add `!important` to the term-failure background-color, or exclude `.term-failure` from the idle `:not(.term-busy):not(.term-needs-input)` background rule, or compose failure so it is not masked by the idle !important background. Add a render/e2e assertion that a failed-only (idle) Orky pane shows the failure accent.
resolution: FIXED: failure CSS now !important and idle rule excludes .term-failure; [data-failed] non-color affordance; pinned by TEST-051 + e2e TEST-055 (green).

claim: Failure is signalled by color alone (WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color). needs-human gets a non-color affordance (the 🔔 prefix on the chip and title), but a `failed` feature gets none: the chip sets `data-failed` (PaneToolbar.tsx:47) yet no CSS targets it, and the chip label/icon is unchanged for a failure. The only failure cue is the red toolbar background (term-failure), which is both color-only and — per the HIGH finding above — frequently suppressed. A colorblind user cannot distinguish a failed Orky pane from an idle/busy one across a wall of terminals.
recommendation: Add a non-color failure marker to the chip (e.g. a ✖ / red glyph prefix, or 'failed' text) the way needs-human adds 🔔, or style the existing `data-failed` attribute with a visible non-color treatment so failure is perceivable without relying on the toolbar color.
resolution: FIXED: non-color failure affordance ([data-failed] glyph + outline) added.

claim: Empty/blank chip for an Orky project with no active features. orkyPaneStatus returns `{ label:'', chipFeature:null }` when the roll-up has no chip feature (src/shared/orky-status.ts), e.g. a `.orky/` whose `features/` dir is empty or all-unreadable (REQ-019 empty roll-up). The toolbar guard is only `{orky && (...chip...)}` (PaneToolbar.tsx:45), so a populated-but-empty OrkyPaneStatus still renders the chip button with text `''` — a contentless, still-clickable bordered box in the toolbar between the proc chip and run chip. Clicking it opens a popover reading 'No active Orky features'. This is a confusing empty state (a mystery blank button) for any Orky repo sitting between runs.
recommendation: Do not render the Orky chip when there is nothing to show: gate it on a non-empty label / `chipFeature !== null` (or have the tracker emit cleared/null for an empty roll-up so the chip is absent rather than blank).
resolution: FIXED: chip only renders on a non-empty chipFeature (empty roll-up no longer blank-chips). Done-project null-label tracked as FINDING-DA-007.

claim: The Orky chip truncates its most actionable content. The chip is `maxWidth:240` with `text-overflow:ellipsis` (PaneToolbar.tsx:48) over the label `feature · phase · gate N/M · ●k open`. Ellipsis clips the END, so on narrow panes in the stated 'wall of terminals' the gate N/M progress and the `●k open` blocking count — the at-a-glance signal the feature exists to provide — are the first things hidden, while the less-actionable feature slug at the start is always kept. The 🔔 needs-you prefix survives, but pipeline progress and open-blocking count do not.
recommendation: Prioritize the actionable tail when space is tight: truncate the feature slug (e.g. middle-ellipsis the slug) rather than the trailing gate/count, or drop the phase word before dropping `●k open`, so N/M and the open count stay visible.

claim: The body-portalled Orky popover has no Escape-to-dismiss and no focus management. It closes only on backdrop click or chip re-toggle (OrkyPopover.tsx:34) — there is no keydown(Escape) handler, focus is not moved into the popover on open, and Tab leaks to the page behind it. A keyboard user can still close it by re-pressing Enter on the still-focused chip (so it is not a hard trap, and it matches the Git/Process popovers), but Escape is the near-universal dismissal affordance for a portalled popover/menu and is absent here.
recommendation: Add a keydown Escape handler that calls onClose (mirroring other dismissible chrome), so the popover is keyboard-dismissable without relying on re-toggling the chip.

claim: The chokidar event handler `onEvt = () => this.schedule(id)` has NO path filter: any add/change/unlink to ANY file under `<root>/.orky/` within depth 4 triggers a full re-read of active.json + every feature's state.json/findings.json. `.orky/features/<slug>/` holds many non-target artifacts (01-concept.md, 02-spec.md, plan/test/findings markdown, intake drafts), so routine edits to spec/plan/doc files during a pipeline tick or doc-sync phase needlessly fire the whole roll-up re-read. This is a concrete regression vs the cloned UsageTracker, whose handler filters `if (p.endsWith('.jsonl'))` (usage-tracker.ts:46) before scheduling.
recommendation: Filter onEvt by basename to the three target files (active.json, state.json, findings.json) before calling schedule(id), or pass chokidar an `ignored` predicate, mirroring UsageTracker's `.jsonl` guard.
resolution: FIXED+verified: chokidar handler isTargetFile() filters to active/state/findings.json before re-read.

claim: Watchers and re-reads are keyed strictly per pane with no dedup across panes that resolve to the same project root. P terminal panes whose cwds live in the same `.orky/` project spawn P independent chokidar watchers on the same `<root>/.orky/` tree, and every `.orky/` event makes each of the P sessions independently readdir(features) + read 1 active.json + 2 files per feature. Cost scales as P x (1 + 2F) file reads per coalesced event for identical data. The spec's REQ-011 'bounded per pane' constrains the emit granularity but does not preclude sharing one watcher+read per root and fanning the result out to each pane.
recommendation: Coalesce by resolved root: maintain one watcher + one debounced re-read per `<root>/.orky/`, then emit the same computed OrkyPaneStatus to every paneId bound to that root. Keeps per-pane emits while collapsing N-fold watcher and file-read amplification.
resolution: FIXED+verified: per-.orky-root dedup (one watcher+read fanned to panes); reference-counting verified correct (last-pane closes, no premature close).

claim: The per-feature read loop in reread() awaits each feature's state.json then findings.json sequentially, so total read latency grows linearly with feature count even though the reads are independent. With many features each coalesced event serializes 2F awaits back-to-back.
recommendation: Resolve the per-feature reads concurrently (e.g. Promise.all over slugs, reading state.json + findings.json per feature in parallel), keeping the post-await session-identity re-check after the batch resolves.

claim: findOrkyRoot uses synchronous `statSync` (up to maxDepth+1 = 9 calls) and is invoked from OrkyTracker.watch() on the main-process event loop on every pane cwd change. A workspace restore that spawns/binds many panes at once issues P x up-to-9 synchronous stat syscalls back-to-back, briefly blocking the main loop that also routes PTY data.
recommendation: Use async `fs.promises.stat` in the bounded upward walk (the caller already awaits watch()), or memoize root resolution per directory, to avoid synchronous fs on the main critical path.

claim: readJson reads each file in full via readFile + JSON.parse with no size bound. findings.json accumulates entries across a long pipeline (multiple reviewer lenses appending), and the whole file is read and parsed on every coalesced event, for every feature, for every pane (compounding with the dedup gap above). There is no mtime gate or size cap, so per-event cost tracks unbounded findings.json growth rather than the small subset of fields actually consumed (status/severity/contract_violation).
recommendation: Gate re-reads on mtime so unchanged files are skipped, and/or cap the read size for findings.json; only re-read the feature(s) whose files actually changed rather than the whole roll-up.

claim: activeLastTick parses active.json.lastTickAt with Date.parse(tick). Per ECMAScript, an ISO date-time form WITHOUT a timezone designator (e.g. "2026-06-30T12:04:00") is interpreted in the machine's LOCAL timezone, while `now` comes from Date.now() (absolute epoch ms). For any tz-less timestamp the computed `now - lastTickAt` shifts by the local UTC offset (potentially hours, plus DST), so the 120s stall decision (isStalled / REQ-003) and the active feature's lastActivityAt ranking (REQ-007) become timezone- and DST-dependent. Two users in different timezones reading the same synced .orky/ tree would compute different stall states from identical bytes. The pure mappers correctly inject `now`, but this normalization boundary reintroduces locale/timezone nondeterminism. (Tz-aware timestamps with 'Z'/offset, e.g. from Date#toISOString, are unaffected.)
recommendation: Do not rely on Date.parse's local-time fallback. Either require lastTickAt to carry an explicit UTC/offset and reject (-> null) a tz-less value, or normalize a tz-less ISO string to UTC before parsing. Add a unit test pinning that a fixed tz-less timestamp yields the same epoch ms regardless of process TZ (run with TZ set to a non-UTC zone).
resolution: FIXED+verified: parseOrkyTimestamp interprets tz-less timestamps as UTC; TEST-042 pins identical-bytes->identical epoch.

claim: TASK-019 (REQ-022) explicitly required reconciling .orky/baseline/architecture.md to note the new feature (the presentation-layer override of byte-status for Orky panes), but the implementation diff (6d52ee9..HEAD) does not touch it. The baseline architecture map — which CLAUDE.md designates as the canonical confirmed system map agents read first — has zero mention of the new privileged main-process subsystem `src/main/orky/` (orky-tracker.ts + find-orky-root.ts doing chokidar watches + fs reads) or the `orky:status` IPC channel, so the map now under-describes the shipped system.
recommendation: Add `src/main/orky/` (read-only `.orky/` watcher) and the `orky:status` channel to the baseline architecture map, and note the render-time precedence of Orky status over byte-derived TerminalStatus for the pane border/tab-badge (REQ-014). Pure reconciliation suitable for the doc-sync phase.
resolution: FIXED: .orky/baseline/architecture.md reconciled (src/main/orky/ subsystem + orky:status channel).

claim: The Orky reference link is a placeholder `[Orky](https://github.com/)` pointing at the GitHub homepage rather than an actual Orky project/repo. The identical placeholder link also appears in the CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] entry. A reader following the link lands on github.com, not the tool being described.
recommendation: Point the link at the real Orky repo/spec URL, or drop the hyperlink and describe Orky in prose. Fix both the doc and the CHANGELOG occurrence.
resolution: FIXED: placeholder github.com links removed from feature doc + CHANGELOG.

claim: 00-intake.md cites its full intake as "verbatim, from `.orky/0004-intake-draft.md`", but that source file is an uncommitted throwaway (it appears as an untracked `?? .orky/0004-intake-draft.md` in git status and is not part of the repo). The provenance pointer therefore references a file a reader cannot find in the checked-in tree.
recommendation: Either remove the dangling reference to the throwaway draft (the content is already inlined verbatim) or commit the draft. Low impact since the verbatim content is present inline.

claim: ORKY_PHASES is VERIFIED to currently match ground truth (the recorded gate keys in 0003-pane-minimize-restore/state.json and Orky's gatekeeper.js DRIVER_WORK_PHASES are both exactly the 8 mirrored phases; gateM=8 and human-review counting are correct). But the drift caveat (orky-status.ts:4-5,10 + docs/features/orky-status.md:70-76) is a provenance trap: it cites only 'Orky's upstream pipeline phase list' without naming the exact source, AND Orky's gatekeeper has a SEPARATE constant literally named 'Canonical phase order' (PHASE_ORDER, C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/gatekeeper.js:28) = ['intake','brainstorm','spec','plan','tests','implement','review','doc-sync','human'] (9 entries: adds 'intake' at front, ends in 'human' not 'human-review'). The Termhalla code comment even calls ORKY_PHASES 'Canonical pipeline phase order' — colliding with the name of Orky's DIFFERENT 9-element list. A maintainer re-verifying the mirror against Orky's literally-canonical constant would wrongly prepend 'intake' (making M=9) and rename human-review->human (zeroing gateN's match on the recorded 'human-review' gate key), silently corrupting every gate N/M.
recommendation: Make the provenance note cite the EXACT mirrored source: the recorded state.json.gates keys + gatekeeper.js DRIVER_WORK_PHASES (line 690), NOT PHASE_ORDER. Explicitly state that Orky's 'Canonical phase order' constant's 'intake' (a pre-pipeline artifact step that records no gate) and 'human' (recorded as 'human-review') are intentionally excluded/renamed, so a future maintainer does not 're-sync' against the wrong list. Rename the local comment off 'Canonical phase order' to avoid the name collision.
resolution: FIXED: ORKY_PHASES provenance comment corrected + drift caveat vs Orky 9-entry PHASE_ORDER; documented.

claim: STALL_THRESHOLD_MS = 120_000 is presented as authoritative ('a live executing phase with no heartbeat for >120s is stalled', orky-status.ts:15; spec REQ-003 mandates 120_000) with no cited source for the 120s value, and it is not reconciled with Orky's own liveness/idle threshold. Orky's gatekeeper liveness() (C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/gatekeeper.js:843) takes idleThresholdSeconds as a CALLER-SUPPLIED argument and defines no canonical value — so the chip's notion of 'stalled' is an independent, uncited Termhalla heuristic that nothing verifies against Orky's actual watchdog threshold. If the real Orky watchdog uses a different idle threshold, the chip can confidently render 'stalled Nm — needs you' (kind:needs-input) while Orky still considers the run live / not yet auto-resumed, surfacing a false call-to-action.
recommendation: Document 120s explicitly as a Termhalla-side UI heuristic (NOT an Orky-derived value), note that Orky's idle threshold is caller-configured with no canonical default, and prefer sourcing the threshold from the same config Orky's watchdog uses rather than an independent private constant. The value is already injectable via thresholdMs, so wire it from configuration instead of hardcoding the user-facing 'stalled' verdict.
resolution: Resolved 2026-07-12 both halves: Orky v0.44.0 (9443bee) gave liveness a canonical caller->config->3600s threshold resolution published in the contract watchdog block; Termhalla dropped the 120s heuristic for resolveStallThresholdMs applying the same config->default resolution per root (engine + detail path; tests/main/orky-stall-threshold.test.ts). Kevin-directed close.

claim: compareFeatures's terminal tiebreak is the feature id, but orkyFeatureStatus (line 188) collapses a missing/empty `feature` field to the constant slug `'(unknown)'` (`f.feature || '(unknown)'`). Two DISTINCT malformed features (e.g. two feature dirs whose state.json omits `feature`, with equal lastActivityAt — both 0 when neither has gate timestamps) therefore compare EQUAL on every key (needsHuman, reasonRank, lastActivityAt, id), so the comparator is no longer a strict total order for them. The pure selector is deterministic only for the array order it is given; the OrkyTracker builds the feature list in `await readdir('features')` order (orky-tracker.ts:149), which is filesystem-/host-dependent and unsorted. V8's stable sort then preserves that readdir order, so the chip/popover ordering (and the `[0]` chip winner) between two equal-ranked '(unknown)' features varies across hosts from identical bytes — the same class of cross-host nondeterminism FINDING-DET-001 closed, surfacing here through the id-collision edge rather than the timestamp boundary. Well-formed features have distinct slugs and are unaffected.
recommendation: Break the final tie on a value that is unique per on-disk feature even when `feature` is missing — e.g. fold the feature directory slug (the readdir entry name, which IS distinct) into OrkyFeatureStatus and use it as the last tiebreak instead of the displayed `feature || '(unknown)'`; or, cheaply, have the tracker sort `slugs` before mapping so the input order fed to orkyPaneStatus is itself deterministic. Add a test: two features each with `feature:''` and equal lastActivityAt are ordered identically regardless of input-array order.

claim: NEW hole introduced by the gate-based rewrite: a CLEAN-done feature is still chip-eligible, and a done feature's live phase is now `null`, producing two defects nothing catches. (1) `selectChipFeature` ranks over the FULL feature list (incl. clean-done/idle), but `inPopover` now EXCLUDES clean-done (the DA-003 fix). So when every feature is done/idle (an Orky project sitting between runs — the exact steady state of THIS repo once 0004 merges: 0001/0002/0003 all clean-done), the chip RENDERS (PaneToolbar gates only on `chipFeature !== null`, and the chip-feature is a clean-done feature) yet clicking it opens an empty `No active Orky features` popover — the chip names a feature its own popover omits. TEST-018's own data (`[doneOpen d1, doneClean d2]`) constructs exactly this: `chipFeature` resolves to d2 (newer activity) while `features` is `['d1']`, but the test only asserts `.features` and never that the chip feature appears in the popover, so it passes green over the mismatch. (2) For a non-active done feature `livePhase = gateFrontier(allPassed) = null` (verified: TEST-009 maps an all-passed feature to done with phase left null, unasserted), and `chipLabel` (`${f.feature} · ${f.phase} · ...`) plus `OrkyFeatureRow` (`{f.phase} · {f.gateN}/{f.gateM}`) do NOT guard null — so the primary chip renders the literal string `"<slug> · null · 8/8"`. Notably the `detail` builder DOES guard every null (`livePhase ?? 'idle'`/`'running'`/`'gate'`), proving the author knew phase can be null and simply missed the chip label and popover row. Before the rewrite, phase came from `state.json.phase` (= `'doc-sync'` for done features) so the chip read `"<slug> · doc-sync · 8/8"` and clean-done features appeared in the popover — both symptoms are regressions the gate-based model introduced.
recommendation: Guard the null live phase in the user-facing renderers exactly as `detail` already does: in `chipLabel` use `f.phase ?? 'done'` (or omit the phase segment when null), and in `OrkyFeatureRow` render `{f.phase ?? 'done'} · …`. Separately, resolve the chip/popover set mismatch: either select the chip ONLY from the popover-eligible (`inPopover`) set so the chip always corresponds to a listed feature, OR have `orkyPaneStatus` return the idle/cleared shape (no chip) when the popover-eligible set is empty so an all-done/idle project shows no chip rather than a `null`-phase done chip over an empty popover. Add a test asserting (a) chipLabel of a done (null-phase) feature contains no literal `'null'`, and (b) `orkyPaneStatus([doneOpen, doneClean]).chipFeature` is present in `.features` (or the roll-up clears).
resolution: FIXED+verified (loopback iter2): chipLabel/OrkyFeatureRow render f.phase ?? "done" (no null leak); orkyPaneStatus selects chip from inPopover-eligible set so chipFeature is null or in .features (chip/popover consistent). Pinned by TEST-018/056/057 (green); devils-advocate+determinism verify PASS, no actionable feature dropped.

claim: The symlink guard introduced for SEC-004 (REQ-025c) calls `lstat(fdir)` on the `features/<slug>` directory entry and skips it if the DIRECTORY is a symlink. It does NOT guard file-level symlinks within a real directory. If an attacker with write access to `<project>/.orky/features/<slug>/` plants a symbolic link named `state.json` or `findings.json` pointing to an arbitrary file outside the project (e.g. `features/real-slug/state.json -> /etc/shadow`), `readFile` will follow that symlink transparently. The contents are then parsed through `normalizeFeatureRaw` / `normalizeFindings` and emitted into the UI. This is not addressed by the current lstat directory check, which only prevents the original attack described in SEC-004 (a symlinked directory entry like `features/evil -> /outside/`). Constraints on impact: (a) the 1 MiB size cap from SEC-003/REQ-025b still applies — a file larger than 1 MiB will be stat'd first and skipped before readFile is called (order is correct); (b) all data reaches the UI via React rendering which escapes text content, so no HTML injection; (c) an attacker needs write access to a non-root subdirectory of the user's own project. The spec (REQ-025c) explicitly offered `lstat + skip` as an alternative to the realpath approach and does not mention file-level symlinks — this is therefore a residual gap in the spec-permitted implementation rather than a coding error against spec.
recommendation: Either (a) extend the per-file guard: before calling `readJson(join(fdir, 'state.json'), ...)`, call `lstat(join(fdir, 'state.json'))` and skip if `isSymbolicLink()` (same pattern already used for the directory); or (b) switch to the realpath approach for the directory (compute `realpath(fdir)` and verify the resolved path still starts with `realpath(orkyDir)`) which simultaneously closes both the directory-level and file-level symlink attack vectors AND eliminates the inherent TOCTOU window between the lstat check and the subsequent readFile calls. Approach (b) is one `realpath` call per feature and more robust. Whichever approach is chosen, add a test fixture reproducing the file-level symlink case (a real directory whose `state.json` entry is a symlink) and assert the content is not read.

claim: The per-root dedup is correct, but the immediate on-bind re-read fans its result out to EVERY pane already bound to the root, not just the joining pane. `watch()` unconditionally calls `await this.reread(orkyDir)` (line 78) when a pane binds, and `reread` emits to `for (const id of r.panes) this.emit(id, status)` (line 185). So when P panes in the same project bind in quick succession (e.g. a workspace restore that spawns many panes resolving to one `.orky/` root), pane k's bind re-reads and re-emits identical status to all k already-bound panes: total emits = 1+2+...+P = O(P^2) safeSend IPC pushes + renderer store updates + chip re-renders, versus the O(P) of the prior per-pane design (each pane re-emitted only to itself). The file-read amplification PERF-002 targeted is fixed; this is a smaller, newly-introduced emit amplification specific to the shared fan-out. Each push is cheap (a small precomputed status object), so impact is bounded and one-time per bind burst, not per ongoing event.
recommendation: On a bind where the root already has a live watcher (the joining pane is not the first), emit only to the newly-joining pane rather than re-reading and fanning out to the whole set. Cache the last computed OrkyPaneStatus on the Root and push it to the new pane directly (still re-reading only when the watcher does not yet exist), so a P-pane restore costs O(P) emits instead of O(P^2).
